title: Console - Serverless Backend
content:
Serverless Backend in the AWS Console (optional) This is a Console configuration map, not the primary deployment method. If the API stack exists, use the Console only for inspection; do not recreate functions, triggers, or APIs with the same names.
1. Execution roles and permissions Create the roles manually Repeat this procedure for ApiHandler, ImageProcessor, and AiAnalyzer:
Open the IAM Console → Roles → Create role. Select AWS service, use case Lambda, and choose Next. Attach the AWS managed policy AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole. Name the roles SmartImage-ApiHandlerRole-staging, SmartImage-ImageProcessorRole-staging, and SmartImage-AiAnalyzerRole-staging. Create the role, open it, and choose Add permissions → Create inline policy. Select JSON, add the actions in the table below, and replace example ARNs with the actual staging resources. Choose Next, name the policy for the function, and choose Create policy. Each Lambda has a separate execution role with AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole and resource-scoped staging permissions:
Lambda Main permissions ApiHandler S3 Get/Put/Delete; DynamoDB Get/Put/Update/Delete/Query/Scan/BatchGet/BatchWrite on three tables and indexes; cognito-idp:AdminUpdateUserAttributes on the User Pool ImageProcessor S3 Get on the raw bucket, Put on the processed bucket; DynamoDB read/write/query on Images; SQS SendMessage for its DLQ AiAnalyzer S3 Get on the raw bucket; DynamoDB stream read and table read/write/batch; Rekognition DetectLabels/DetectModerationLabels; SQS SendMessage for its DLQ Difference from CDK: CDK creates roles and resource grants automatically. The custom managed policy in the screenshot is only a manual equivalent. Restrict resource ARNs to the actual buckets, tables, indexes, streams, queues, and User Pool.
In policy JSON, include both the table ARN and table/<name>/index/* for GSI queries. Use table/<name>/stream/* for streams and end S3 object ARNs with /*. Do not use Resource: "*" for S3, DynamoDB, or Cognito simply to make the lab work.
Create two dead-letter queues Open the Amazon SQS Console → Create queue. Select Standard. Create SmartImage-ImageProcessorDlq-staging, keep default encryption, and set message retention to 14 days. Repeat for SmartImage-AiAnalyzerDlq-staging. Record both queue ARNs for sqs:SendMessage permissions and on-failure destinations. 2. Lambda functions The Console walkthrough focuses on three business functions:
Function Architecture Memory Timeout Temporary storage SmartImage-ApiHandler-staging ARM64 512 MB 15 seconds Default SmartImage-ImageProcessor-staging ARM64 1536 MB 120 seconds 1024 MB SmartImage-AiAnalyzer-staging ARM64 512 MB 60 seconds Default The deployment screenshot shows Node.js 20.x because that is the runtime in the current CDK code. The current Create function screen offers Node.js 24.x; a manual function can use it after package compatibility is tested. Selecting a newer runtime in the Console does not update the CDK code.
Long names such as CustomS3AutoDeleteObject, BucketNotificationsHandler, and LogRetention are CDK provider functions. SmartImage-Authorizer-staging also exists, but the API uses the Cognito User Pool Authorizer.
Create functions in the Console For each business function:
Open the AWS Lambda Console → Functions → Create function. Select Author from scratch. Under Basic information, enter the exact Function name from the table above. For Runtime, select Node.js 24.x or another supported Node.js runtime tested with the package. Expand Additional settings under Custom settings. Turn on ARM64 architecture. Turn on Custom execution role. The Configure custom execution role panel opens on the right. For Execution role, select Choose an existing role, select the matching role, and choose Save in the panel. Leave Durable execution, EC2 capacity provider, Function URL, VPC, and other settings at their defaults because CDK does not use them. Scroll to the bottom and choose Create function. After creation, open Configuration → General configuration → Edit and set memory and timeout from the table. For ImageProcessor, set Ephemeral storage to 1024 MB. Open Configuration → Environment variables → Edit and enter the function values. Deployment package Do not zip raw TypeScript source. Build with esbuild/NodejsFunction; ImageProcessor must include Sharp compiled for Linux ARM64. AWS SDK v3 can be externalized as in CDK or bundled deliberately.
To create a package equivalent to CDK:
Install dependencies and run the project build/tests before packaging. Bundle each entry point with esbuild for the node platform, ARM64 architecture, and target runtime. Verify that the configured handler is exported under the expected name, such as index.handler. For ImageProcessor, install or bundle a linux-arm64 Sharp binary; a Windows Sharp package will not run on Lambda Linux. Zip the contents of the output directory, not an extra parent directory. On the Code tab, choose Upload from → .zip file, upload the matching package, and choose Save. For a package above the direct-upload limit, upload through S3 or use a layer/container image. That is an optional variation, not the current CDK configuration. Environment variables All three functions use IMAGE_TABLE_NAME, USER_QUOTA_TABLE_NAME, USER_PROFILE_TABLE_NAME, RAW_BUCKET_NAME, PROCESSED_BUCKET_NAME, ENVIRONMENT=staging, POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME=SmartImage, POWERTOOLS_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG, and NODE_OPTIONS=--enable-source-maps.
Additional values:
ApiHandler: USER_POOL_ID, PRESIGNED_URL_EXPIRY=900. ImageProcessor: THUMBNAIL_WIDTH=200, THUMBNAIL_HEIGHT=200, RESIZED_MAX_WIDTH=1920, RESIZED_MAX_HEIGHT=1080. AiAnalyzer: RAW_BUCKET_NAME and IMAGE_TABLE_NAME are required for image reads and metadata updates. Use actual physical resource names rather than logical names. After saving, open Configuration → Permissions and verify the execution role. Run a small test event or inspect the log group to confirm that the function initializes before adding triggers.
The ImageProcessor example contains 15 variables. AI_ANALYZER_FUNCTION_NAME is empty in the current CDK deployment because DynamoDB Streams invokes AiAnalyzer; ImageProcessor does not invoke it directly.
3. S3 Event Notification Open the raw bucket in the Amazon S3 Console. Choose Properties and scroll to Event notifications. Choose Create event notification. Enter InvokeImageProcessor-staging as the name. Enter prefix users/ and leave suffix empty. Under Event types, select All object create events. Under Destination, select Lambda function and SmartImage-ImageProcessor-staging. Choose Save changes. S3 adds the resource-based permission required to invoke Lambda. The result must be:
Event: All object create events. Prefix: users/. Destination: SmartImage-ImageProcessor-staging. Do not add a suffix when Lambda is responsible for format validation. The screenshot illustrates the destination only; enter the event type and prefix listed above.
4. DynamoDB Stream event source Verify that SmartImage-Images-staging has a New and old images stream. Open the table → Exports and streams → DynamoDB stream details. Choose Create trigger, or open SmartImage-AiAnalyzer-staging → Add trigger → DynamoDB. Select the Images table stream ARN. Set Batch size to 10 and enable the trigger. Choose Add/Create trigger. After creation, open the event source mapping in Lambda and verify/configure:
Starting position: TRIM_HORIZON. Maximum retry attempts: 3. On-failure destination: SmartImage-AiAnalyzerDlq-staging SQS queue. Trigger enabled. The screenshot shows only the initial trigger form; CDK also declares retry and destination settings.
If retry and destination are absent from the DynamoDB form, open Lambda → Configuration → Triggers, select the event source mapping, and choose Edit. Grant stream-read permission to the role before enabling the mapping, or the trigger will enter an error state.
5. API Gateway and Cognito Authorizer Create the REST API Open the API Gateway Console → Create API. Under REST API, choose Build. Do not choose HTTP API because CDK uses REST API v1. Select New API, enter SmartImage-API-staging, choose Regional, and choose Create API. Create the Cognito authorizer In the new API, choose Authorizers → Create authorizer. Enter CognitoAuth and select type Cognito. Select Region ap-southeast-1 and User Pool SmartImage-UserPool-staging. Enter Authorization as the Token source and leave token validation empty. Choose Create authorizer. Create resources and methods Choose Resources → Create resource and begin with /v1. Create the nested resource tree. Preserve braces in {imageId} so API Gateway treats it as a path parameter. Select the target resource and choose Create method. Under Method details, select the required Method type, such as GET, POST, PATCH, or DELETE. For Integration type, select Lambda function. Turn on Lambda proxy integration so the complete request is passed to ApiHandler as a structured event. For Response transfer mode, select Buffered; this project does not use response streaming. Under Lambda function, select Region ap-southeast-1, then find and select SmartImage-ApiHandler-staging. Keep the default timeout unless another value is required. When saving, allow API Gateway to add invoke permission to the Lambda resource policy. Choose Create method. Open the new method → Method request tab → Edit. For a protected route, select Authorization/Authorizer: CognitoAuth. Keep None for GET /v1/images/public. For PATCH/POST, select the request validator equivalent to CDK and save the method request. Repeat according to this table: Method Path Authorization GET, PATCH /v1/profile CognitoAuth POST /v1/profile/avatar/presigned-url CognitoAuth GET /v1/images CognitoAuth POST /v1/images/presigned-url CognitoAuth GET /v1/images/public NONE DELETE /v1/images/bulk CognitoAuth GET /v1/images/search CognitoAuth GET, DELETE, PATCH /v1/images/{imageId} CognitoAuth GET /v1/images/{imageId}/download CognitoAuth GET /v1/admin/moderation CognitoAuth plus group check in Lambda POST /v1/admin/moderation/{imageId} CognitoAuth plus group check in Lambda Enable CORS on resources called by the frontend; allow the Amplify origin, Content-Type,Authorization headers, and only the methods used. Choose Deploy API, create stage dev, and deploy. Copy the invoke URL: https://<api-id>.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/dev. Call GET /v1/images/public without a token to verify the public route; a protected route without a token should return 401 Unauthorized. In the current CDK code, the staging environment maps to API Gateway stage dev; production uses prod.
CDK currently also declares /v1/auth/signup, /login, and /refresh, but the Lambda router has no matching handlers; the frontend authenticates directly with Cognito. Do not use those three routes in the Console walkthrough.
6. AWS WAF Web ACL (optional Console path) CDK creates a Regional AWS WAF v2 Web ACL and associates it with API Gateway stage dev. WAF evaluates requests before the Cognito authorizer, so a WAF-blocked request never reaches authentication or Lambda.
AWS WAF has separate charges for Web ACLs, rules, and requests. Inspect the CDK-created resource instead of creating a manual duplicate when extra lab cost is not wanted.
Create the Web ACL The new Console labels a Web ACL as a Protection pack (web ACL). This is a UI naming change; CDK and the API still use WebACL.
Open the AWS WAF Console → Protection packs (web ACLs). Check Region scope, select ap-southeast-1, and choose Create protection pack (web ACL). Under Tell us about your app, select API & integration services. Media & file processing may also be selected, but it only affects Console recommendations and is not present in CDK. Expand Select resources to protect, select add resources -> Add regional resources, API SmartImage-API-staging, and stage dev. Under Choose initial protections, retain or add only the rules required to match CDK; other Console-recommended protection packages are optional. Expand Name and describe, enter SmartImage-ApiWebAcl-staging and CloudWatch metric name SmartImage-WafMetrics-staging. Keep the default body inspection size and default action Allow; CDK does not increase body inspection. Continue to review and create the protection pack. Add AWS Managed Rules Choose Add rules → Add managed rule groups. Expand AWS managed rule groups and enable Core rule set (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet). Keep rule-group actions (Use rule actions/None) to match CDK overrideAction: none. Save it with priority 1 and enable CloudWatch metrics/sampled requests. Outside the lab, the rule group can first run in Count mode to observe false positives. That operational choice differs from the current CDK configuration. Add the rate-based rule Choose Add rules → Add my own rules and rule groups → Rule builder. Select Rate-based rule and enter RateLimitRule. Aggregate by Source IP address. Enter rate limit 2000, use the 5 minutes evaluation window (the default when CDK omits the value), and choose Block. Set priority 2, metric name RateLimitRuleMetric, and enable CloudWatch metrics/sampled requests. Keep the Web ACL default action Allow and choose Create web ACL. Verify association Open API Gateway → SmartImage-API-staging → Stages → dev → Edit. Under Web application firewall (AWS WAF), select the new Web ACL and save. Return to WAF → Web ACL → Associated AWS resources and verify that stage dev appears. Inspect Sampled requests and both rule metrics after the API receives traffic. 7. CloudWatch, alarms, and SNS (optional Console path) API Gateway access logs Open CloudWatch → Logs → Log management → Create log group. Enter /aws/apigateway/SmartImage-staging, select 30-day retention, and log class Standard. Leave KMS key empty for service-managed encryption; deletion protection is optional and is not enabled by the current CDK stack. Open API Gateway → API → Stages → dev → Logs and tracing/Edit. Enable access logging, select the log-group ARN, and use a JSON format with standard fields such as request ID, IP, caller, HTTP method, resource path, status, protocol, response length, and request time. CDK also enables X-Ray tracing for the stage. Turn on X-Ray tracing for an equivalent Console configuration. SNS topic and email subscription Open the Amazon SNS Console → Topics → Create topic. Select Standard, name SmartImage-Alarms-staging, and display name SmartImage staging Alarms. Keep Encryption, Access policy, and Delivery policy at their defaults for the lab, then choose Create topic. In the new topic, choose Create subscription, protocol Email, and enter the notification address. Open the AWS Notification email and choose Confirm subscription. A Pending confirmation subscription receives no alarm notifications. Create alarms equivalent to CDK In CloudWatch, choose Alarms → All alarms → Create alarm. In Step 1 Specify metric and conditions, select data source Metrics, type Classic, and choose Select metric. In the metric dialog, choose the Lambda, ApiGateway, or DynamoDB namespace and the dimension shown below. Configure statistic, period, threshold, and missing-data handling. In Step 2 Configure actions, select In alarm and send a notification to SmartImage-Alarms-staging. In Step 3, use a name prefixed SmartImage-staging-; review Step 4 and choose Create alarm. Group Metric and dimension Statistic / Period Condition Each Lambda AWS/Lambda → Errors, FunctionName Sum / 5 minutes >5, 1 period Each Lambda AWS/Lambda → Duration, FunctionName p95 / 5 minutes ApiHandler >12000 ms; ImageProcessor >96000 ms; AiAnalyzer >48000 ms, 2 periods Each Lambda AWS/Lambda → Throttles, FunctionName Sum / 5 minutes >=1, 1 period API Gateway AWS/ApiGateway → 5XXError, ApiName Sum / 5 minutes >10, 1 period API Gateway AWS/ApiGateway → Latency, ApiName p95 / 5 minutes >3000 ms, 2 periods Images DynamoDB AWS/DynamoDB → ThrottledRequests, TableName Sum / 5 minutes >=1, 1 period Set missing data to Treat missing data as not breaching. Prefix alarm names with SmartImage-staging-; the Errors alarm requires at least six errors in one period because the comparison is Greater than 5.
Create the operational dashboard Choose Dashboards → Create dashboard and enter SmartImage-staging-Operations. Add a text widget for the heading. For each Lambda, add an Invocations & Errors graph: Invocations/Sum on the left axis and Errors/Sum on the right, period five minutes. For each Lambda, add a Duration (ms) graph with Average and p95, period five minutes. Arrange three graphs per row and choose Save dashboard. Dashboards are global within an account, but their metric widgets still read a Region; verify that widgets use ap-southeast-1. The WAF and CloudWatch steps are Console illustrations. When CDK has already deployed the stacks, inspect and compare the existing resources instead of creating functional duplicates.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Note: The content below is for reference purposes only. Please do not copy it verbatim into your report.
This section presents my personal evaluation and feedback after participating in the First Cloud AI Journey program. These comments are provided with the hope of improving the learning and internship experience for future participants.
General Evaluation 1. Learning and Working Environment
The First Cloud AI Journey environment was friendly, open, and supportive of independent learning. During the program, I was able to discuss technical difficulties and practical exercises with mentors and other members whenever I needed assistance.
The learning materials were organized by topic, which made it easier for me to follow my progress. However, I believe the program could organize more networking sessions or group activities so that members have more opportunities to get to know one another, exchange experiences, and provide better support.
2. Support from Mentors and Team Administrators
The mentors and team administrators provided active support throughout my participation in the program. When I encountered a problem, I was guided to identify the cause, search for relevant documentation, and work through the solution independently instead of simply receiving a ready-made answer.
This approach helped me improve my self-learning and problem-solving abilities. The team administrators also regularly provided announcements, documents, and necessary instructions, making it easier for members to follow the program.
3. Relevance to My Major
The program content was relevant to the Information Technology and Cybersecurity major that I am currently studying. Topics related to AWS, computer networking, security, system monitoring, access management, and cloud computing helped me reinforce the knowledge I had learned at university.
In addition, I was introduced to new areas such as serverless computing, containers, artificial intelligence, Infrastructure as Code, and practical AWS services.
4. Opportunities for Learning and Skill Development
During the program, I improved several skills, including:
Searching for and reading technical documentation. Using the AWS Management Console and AWS CLI at a basic level. Tracking progress and writing weekly worklogs. Analyzing errors and performing basic troubleshooting steps. Writing technical documents and reports. Working independently and managing time. Communicating and presenting problems more clearly. The events and sharing sessions also helped me gain more knowledge about Cloud, DevOps, Cybersecurity, Artificial Intelligence, and career development in the IT industry.
5. Culture and Supportive Team Spirit
The members of the program demonstrated a strong willingness to share knowledge and support one another. Participants could discuss lessons, challenges encountered during practice, and their experiences using AWS services.
I appreciated the serious yet friendly learning environment. It made me feel more comfortable asking questions and discussing topics that I did not fully understand.
6. Learning Materials and Activities for Interns
The program provided a wide range of learning materials and workshops related to AWS. The content was divided into sections, allowing beginners to start with fundamental knowledge before moving on to more advanced topics.
In addition to technical content, the program also included community events, experience-sharing sessions, and career orientation activities. These activities were valuable for students preparing to enter a professional working environment.
Additional Questions What was I most satisfied with during the program?
The aspect I was most satisfied with was the opportunity to learn about AWS services through specific materials and practical exercises. I not only studied the theory but also worked directly with the AWS Console, checked resources, and recorded my progress each week.
In addition, the support from mentors and the community gave me more motivation to maintain my learning progress and complete the assigned content.
What should the program improve for future interns?
In my opinion, the program could improve several areas:
Provide clearer learning roadmaps for different directions such as Cloud, DevOps, Security, or AI. Organize more live practical sessions with mentors. Include more group assignments or small projects so that members can collaborate. Provide specific evaluation criteria for each week. Organize more sessions about CV preparation, interviews, and practical working experience. Update or clearly note workshops where the AWS interface has changed compared to the documentation. Would I recommend the program to my friends?
I would recommend the program to friends who are interested in AWS, cloud computing, and IT-related career paths.
The program is suitable for students who are willing to learn independently, gain practical knowledge, and spend time completing exercises. However, participants should be proactive because some topics require additional research and troubleshooting during practice.
Suggestions and Expectations Organize regular question-and-answer sessions between mentors and interns. Provide workshops on CV preparation, GitHub portfolio development, and interview preparation. Include a final presentation activity where members can share the knowledge or projects they have completed. I would like to continue participating in First Cloud AI Journey activities in the future to gain more knowledge about AWS, Cloud, DevOps, and Cybersecurity.
Overall, the program provided me with valuable knowledge and experience. Participating in the program helped me improve my self-learning ability, gain a better understanding of AWS services, and identify the skills that I need to continue developing for my future career.

title: Console - Amplify Hosting
content:
React Frontend on AWS Amplify (optional) The Frontend stack creates the Amplify app, branch, build specification, environment variables, and SPA rewrite through CDK. This section shows the Console equivalent.
1. Connect the repository Push the tested source to the repository and staging branch before opening Amplify. Open the AWS Amplify Console in ap-southeast-1. Choose Create new app → Host web app or Deploy an app, depending on the current layout. Select GitHub as the source provider and choose Next/Continue. When prompted, choose Install & Authorize AWS Amplify and grant the GitHub App access to the required repository. Use Only select repositories to limit scope when possible. Return to Amplify and select repository AWS-Project and branch staging. Enable monorepo support if shown and enter app root frontend. If the build specification below runs from the repository root, inspect the preview to avoid a duplicated frontend/frontend path. Choose Next to open build settings. The screenshot illustrates GitHub App authorization only; select the repository and branch for the target environment.
2. Environment variables In App settings/Build settings, expand Environment variables. Choose Add variable and configure four variables: Key Value VITE_API_URL Invoke URL for the staging API environment (currently ending in /dev) VITE_USER_POOL_ID Cognito User Pool ID VITE_CLIENT_ID Cognito app client ID VITE_AWS_REGION ap-southeast-1 Avoid an unintended trailing / in the API base URL. Never place secrets in VITE_* variables because their values are embedded in browser-delivered JavaScript.
Copy the API URL from API Gateway stage dev and the IDs from Cognito rather than inferring them from display names. Verify that the values apply to staging. Do not copy production values into the staging branch. After creation, edit values under Hosting → Environment variables or App settings → Environment variables, then redeploy the branch. 3. Build specification CDK sets the build specification directly on the Amplify app. The Console equivalent is:
Under Build settings, choose Edit or Edit YML. Replace auto-detected content with the configuration below when it does not detect the monorepo correctly. version: 1 frontend: phases: preBuild: commands: - npm ci build: commands: - npm run --workspace=frontend build artifacts: baseDirectory: frontend/dist files: - '**/*' cache: paths: - node_modules/**/* When the repository uses amplify.yml, keep it equivalent to the build specification above; avoid maintaining two conflicting sources of build configuration.
Choose Save and verify npm ci, the frontend workspace command, and output directory frontend/dist. Do not change baseDirectory to dist unless app root is already frontend and the build log confirms commands run from that directory. Choose Next, enter an app name with a staging suffix if requested, and review the settings. 4. SPA rewrite and deployment Choose Save and deploy to start the first build. Open the build and follow Provision, Build, Deploy, and Verify. If it fails, inspect the first relevant error in the log before retrying. After deployment, open Rewrites and redirects in app settings. Add an SPA-regex source that excludes static files, target /index.html, type 200 (Rewrite). Do not use 301/302 for React routes; the browser must receive index.html while preserving the route URL. Save the rule, open the branch URL, navigate directly to a client-side route, and refresh. In DevTools → Network, verify requests use the /dev API URL, protected requests include Authorization, and no CORS error occurs. 5. Verify automatic builds Push a small change to branch staging. Confirm that Amplify starts a new build for the correct branch. Check that the build commit ID matches the pushed commit. If automatic deployment is not wanted after the lab, disable Auto build. This is an operational choice and differs from the current CDK default. The CloudFront distribution behind Amplify is managed by the service and is not a distribution for the processed S3 bucket. The project’s Storage stack still has its separate CloudFront distribution disabled.

title: Product Showcase
content:
Smart Image Platform in the live environment This section presents the deployed Smart Image Platform running on a real domain. The screenshots are extracted from the system demonstration video rather than mocked AWS Console data.
Production can use branch, stage, and resource names that differ from the staging Console walkthrough. The application architecture and core processing flow still correspond to the CDK project.
Access information Item Link Application https://www.minphstudio.com/ Source code hngluc/AWS-Project Demo video Google Drive video folder The full demonstration is approximately 2 minutes 49 seconds and covers registration, email verification, upload, AI processing, editing, search, and image sharing.
1. Sign-in and Community Gallery access The application uses Amazon Cognito for registration, email confirmation, and sign-in. Visitors can choose View Community Gallery without signing in; My Gallery, upload, and management operations require an authenticated session.
2. Direct upload and asynchronous processing The Upload screen accepts drag-and-drop or selected JPEG, PNG, WEBP, and GIF files up to 25 MB. After the API issues a presigned URL, the browser uploads the file directly to the raw S3 bucket.
The Processing Queue displays each file’s progress. In the screenshot, upload has completed and the file is in AI Processing. The Connected to AWS badge confirms that the frontend is using the AWS backend rather than demo mode.
The flow behind the interface is:
API Handler creates metadata and a presigned PUT URL. The browser uploads the original directly to Amazon S3. An S3 Event invokes Image Processor to generate resized output, a thumbnail, and EXIF metadata. DynamoDB Streams invokes AiAnalyzer. Amazon Rekognition produces object and moderation labels. Final metadata is updated so the frontend can display COMPLETED. 3. Image Insights and AI labels After the pipeline completes, Image Insights & Controls combines processing state, visibility, upload time, size, EXIF, and detected objects. Each AI label includes a confidence score to distinguish stronger and weaker matches.
From this view, a user can:
Switch an image between PRIVATE and PUBLIC. Download the original through an expiring presigned URL. Open the basic editor or WebGL Shader Lab. Delete the image and its related objects when no longer needed. 4. Image editing and Save as Copy The basic editor provides brightness, contrast, saturation, ±90° rotation, and horizontal/vertical flipping. Save as Copy creates a new image instead of overwriting the original, preserving the initial gallery asset.
5. WebGL Shader Lab WebGL Shader Lab can stack up to five GPU effects. Available effects include grayscale, color tint, pixelation, chromatic aberration, wave distortion, VHS/glitch, halftone, kaleidoscope, and water ripple. Effect order and parameters can be adjusted before saving the result as a copy.
Editing runs in the frontend. A new stored image is created only after Save as Copy is selected.
6. Managing images in My Gallery My Gallery displays images owned by the signed-in account together with processing state and visibility. Users can open details, select multiple images for bulk deletion, refresh processing state, or continue editing a completed image.
Thumbnails and previews use expiring URLs; the raw and processed buckets do not require public-read policies.
7. AI label search AI Tag Search uses labels written to metadata by Amazon Rekognition. In the video, the query city returns images carrying that label without requiring manual tagging during upload.
Search results continue to enforce visibility and access rules so another account’s private images are not exposed.
8. Community Gallery Community Gallery contains PUBLIC images that completed processing and satisfy moderation conditions. Visitors can browse it without authentication, while visibility changes and deletion remain authenticated owner operations.
Demonstrated outcome The live deployment verifies the end-to-end path from the frontend to the AWS backend:
Cognito registration, email verification, and authenticated sessions. Direct S3 uploads through presigned URLs. Lambda/Sharp thumbnail, resized-image, and EXIF processing. Amazon Rekognition labels and moderation analysis. My Gallery, Community Gallery, AI Tag Search, and visibility controls. Basic editing, WebGL Shader Lab, download, and bulk deletion. The next Testing and Verification section explains how to correlate these operations with S3, DynamoDB, Lambda Logs, CloudWatch metrics, and alarms.

title: Testing and Verification
content:
Test Smart Image Platform The following steps use the CDK staging deployment. Substitute equivalent names when resources were created manually.
1. User authentication Open the Amplify branch URL or a custom domain pointing to Amplify. Sign up with an email, full name, and a password satisfying the configured policy. Open the Cognito email, enter the confirmation code, and complete registration. For group testing, open Cognito Console → User Pool → Users, select the account, and add it to user or admin. Sign in again after assigning the group so a new token contains cognito:groups. In DevTools → Network, verify that protected requests include Authorization; do not expose the token in workshop screenshots. 2. Image upload and processing Select a valid JPEG or PNG in Upload Images. Choose Upload and verify that presigned URL generation succeeds. Confirm that the browser PUTs the object directly to the raw bucket; API Gateway/Lambda does not receive the full image binary. Open the raw bucket and verify the object under users/<user-id>/. Follow the image status in the UI; processing is asynchronous, so poll until a terminal state is reached. In the processed bucket, inspect resized/ and thumbnails/ under users/<user-id>/. Compare original, resized, and thumbnail object sizes to verify that Sharp produced outputs rather than copying the file. 3. Metadata and Rekognition Open DynamoDB Console → Tables → SmartImage-Images-staging. Choose Explore table items and locate the item for the uploaded user/image. Open the item in view mode and inspect: PK/SK follow the access pattern. status reflects pipeline progress. thumbnailKey/resizedKey point to the processed bucket. aiTags, moderation labels/status, and EXIF are present after their respective steps complete. Use the screen for observation only; do not edit aiTags manually.
If image-processing metadata exists but AI labels do not, inspect the DynamoDB stream, AiAnalyzer event source mapping, and logs before attributing the problem to Rekognition. 4. Frontend gallery Return to the frontend and open My Gallery. Refresh or wait for the next poll, then select the processed image. Inspect its preview, state, metadata, and AI labels. Open/download the image; the returned value should be an expiring presigned GET URL, not a permanent public S3 URL. Open Community Gallery and verify that GET /v1/images/public does not require a token. Sign out and open My Gallery; the protected route should require authentication. 5. Logs, metrics, and alarms Open CloudWatch Console → Logs → Log groups. Open the latest stream for each Lambda, match its timestamp to the upload, and find the same request/image ID. Inspect API access logs for method, path, and status code. Open Dashboards → SmartImage-staging-Operations and select a time range covering the test. Inspect: Lambda log groups corresponding to SmartImage-ApiHandler-staging, SmartImage-ImageProcessor-staging, and SmartImage-AiAnalyzer-staging. API access log group /aws/apigateway/SmartImage-staging. Dashboard SmartImage-staging-Operations. SNS topic SmartImage-Alarms-staging; confirm the email subscription before expecting notifications. SQS queues SmartImage-ImageProcessorDlq-staging and SmartImage-AiAnalyzerDlq-staging. The Lambda Errors alarm uses a five-minute Sum, threshold 5, and GreaterThanThreshold; at least six errors in one period are required to enter ALARM.
A successful invocation does not prove that alarms or DLQs work. Both require an error that the handler does not swallow, and the alarm also requires enough failures to exceed its threshold.
6. Failure testing A. Frontend validation Select an unsupported extension such as .txt. Verify that the frontend rejects it before upload. Confirm that the raw bucket has no new object. This does not create a Lambda error and cannot test the alarm. B. Backend validation In staging only, upload an object with an image extension but corrupt content directly under users/<test-user>/ in the raw bucket. Open ImageProcessor logs and verify that validation/Sharp reports the failure. Confirm that the test item is not shown as a valid COMPLETED image. C. Current test limitation ImageProcessor and AiAnalyzer currently catch errors without rethrowing in some paths. When Lambda treats the invocation as successful, the Errors metric, retries, and DLQ do not behave like an unhandled failure. Report SNS/DLQ validation as successful only after correcting error propagation or running a separate controlled alarm test.
7. Record evidence Record the timestamp, image ID, API status, raw/processed keys, DynamoDB state, and matching log stream. Distinguish observed results from expected results; do not report an alarm or DLQ as successful without Console evidence.
Do not generate repeated failures in production. Remove test objects and items after validation.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
Resource Cleanup Delete resources only from the intended account, Region, and environment. Do not destroy production before reviewing data-retention requirements.
A. CDK-managed resources From the AWS-Project root, verify the staging stack list:
npm run --workspace=infrastructure cdk -- list -c environment=staging Then run:
npm run --workspace=infrastructure destroy -- -c environment=staging Review the CDK stack list and verify that every name ends in staging. Confirm when CDK prompts. Follow CloudFormation Events. If a stack reaches DELETE_FAILED, inspect its first failed event to identify the dependency. In staging, both S3 buckets use autoDeleteObjects and DESTROY, so CDK can empty and remove them during destruction. Manual emptying is unnecessary unless the deployment was changed or the custom resource fails.
After destruction, inspect CloudFormation and the relevant services to verify that all six stacks were deleted. Do not manually delete stack resources while destroy is still running.
B. Resources created manually in the Console cdk destroy does not remove resources created separately in the Console. Delete them in dependency-aware order.
1. Amplify and WAF Open Amplify Console, select the staging app, choose App settings → Delete app, and complete the confirmation. Open WAF Console and select the Web ACL in ap-southeast-1. Remove its API Gateway stage association, then delete the Web ACL. Do not remove a Web ACL shared by another application. 2. API Gateway and triggers Open API Gateway, select SmartImage-API-staging, verify the API ID, and choose Delete. Open the raw S3 bucket → Properties → Event notifications and delete the ImageProcessor notification. Open the AiAnalyzer Lambda → Configuration → Triggers and delete the DynamoDB event source mapping. Verify that no enabled mapping remains before deleting the function. 3. Lambda, logs, and queues In Lambda Console, delete the three manually created business functions. Delete long-name provider functions only when they are known to belong to this lab/stack. In CloudWatch Logs, delete the three function log groups and /aws/apigateway/SmartImage-staging if audit logs do not need retention. In SQS, delete SmartImage-ImageProcessorDlq-staging and SmartImage-AiAnalyzerDlq-staging after reviewing or exporting messages needed for analysis. 4. DynamoDB and S3 In DynamoDB, select each of the three staging tables. Review backup/PITR requirements, create an on-demand backup if data must be retained, and choose Delete table. For the processed bucket, choose Empty, complete the confirmation, and then Delete bucket. For the versioned raw bucket, use Empty to remove current objects, object versions, and delete markers before deleting the bucket. If Empty does not finish, enable Show versions and inspect remaining versions/delete markers. 5. Cognito, monitoring, and IAM Open the correct staging User Pool in Cognito, ensure no other app uses it, and choose Delete. Its app client and groups are removed with the pool. Delete CloudWatch dashboards and alarms prefixed SmartImage-staging. Delete SNS subscriptions and then topic SmartImage-Alarms-staging. Delete the three workshop IAM roles after Lambda no longer uses them; delete only inline/custom policies created for the lab. Do not delete AdministratorAccess, service-linked roles, or policies used by other resources. C. Post-cleanup verification Recheck ap-southeast-1 and any other Region used during the workshop. Search for resources with the SmartImage prefix/tag and staging environment. Inspect CloudFormation stacks in DELETE_FAILED. Check S3 object versions, CloudWatch Logs, SQS, SNS, WAF, and Amplify. Monitor Billing/Cost Explorer over the following days because cost data can be delayed. Resource Explorer or Resource Groups Tag Editor can help search for SmartImage/staging, but also inspect S3, CloudWatch Logs, and retained resources directly because search results may not include every service immediately.
Production applies RETAIN to selected buckets, tables, and the User Pool. cdk destroy does not remove retained resources. Do not claim zero cost until Billing confirms it and no out-of-stack resources remain.

title: Week 12 Worklog
content:
Week 12 Objectives: Complete an asynchronous image-processing workflow based on an event-driven architecture on AWS.
Integrate Amazon S3, AWS Lambda, DynamoDB Streams, and Amazon Rekognition to automatically process, analyze, and store image information.
Test the entire workflow, monitor execution logs, and clean up the practice resources.
